The Fund exhibited strong relative performance compared to its primary index, the S&P MidCap 400 Growth Index. The operational performances of the Fund's holdings have been robust, and if this strength continues, it should drive wealth creation over the long term. In the short term, stock prices can fluctuate based on macroeconomic sentiment rather than business fundamentals. The first quarter ended with the worst investor sentiment since the early days of COVID, driven by uncertainty surrounding the tariffs announced by the US on April 2nd. The future remains uncertain, and it is unclear whether these tariffs will remain in place or if they are just a starting point in negotiations. That said, the Fund's holdings are likely well-suited to weather a high-tariff environment and possible trade wars that result.

Notably, 57% of the Fund is invested in international companies, with only one directly exposed to US tariffs. Hermes, which generates 15% of its revenue from the Americas, has customers who can easily absorb tariff-induced price increases.

The Fund's managers seek to invest in companies with durable, forecastable, and growing earnings. Such companies often have strong competitive advantages that should help them weather tariff-induced economic challenges. Detailed below are several of the advantages that our Fund holdings may benefit from.

Alternative Asset Managers ("Alt. Managers"): Alt. Managers generate earnings from fees on their assets under management. Fund holding Apollo Global Management (APO) generates 79% of its fee-related earnings from perpetual capital vehicles or long-lived strategies. Another holding, Brookfield Corporation (BN), generates 88% of its fee-related earnings from perpetual capital vehicles or long-lived strategies. Fees should be flowing into these two companies for a long time, regardless of any macroeconomic challenges.

Subscription Business Models: eDreams ODIGEO SA (OTCPK:EDDRF), the European online travel agency, generates most of its earnings from subscription fees. When COVID lockdowns ended in the summer of 2021, eDreams had 1 mm subscribers. Today, it has over 7 mm subscribers. eDreams grew its subscriber base despite the Omicron COVID variant, the invasion of Ukraine, war in the Middle East, and high global inflation, which was particularly acute in Europe. Europeans highly prioritize travel, and eDreams' subscription program helps their customers save money.

Geographic Advantaged Businesses: GFL Environmental, Inc. (GFL) is a municipal waste company. It is usually the dominant player in its markets, which gives it the best route density and the lowest cost. It also owns several landfills, each one typically a local monopoly. SECURE Waste Infrastructure Corp. (OTCPK:SECYF) primarily processes and disposes wastewater from oil production in Western Canada. Solids from the wastewater are disposed of in industrial landfills while the remaining liquids are placed in disposal wells. SECURE controls approximately 70% of the wastewater disposal capacity in Western Canada with minimal geographic overlap with competitors. Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL) and LandBridge Company LLC (LB) have similar advantages with their wastewater disposal wells in the Permian Basin. UK-based SigmaRoc PLC mines limestone in the UK, the Nordics, and northern mainland Europe, holding the number one or two position in each of its markets. Limestone mines are like landfills, typically being the only operator in their geographic area. Furthermore, limestone is a key ingredient in steel manufacturing, which should benefit from the rebuilding of Europe's defense industry.

Secularly Growing Businesses: Chemed Corporation (CHE) owns two businesses: the acyclical Roto Rooter plumbing business and Vitas Healthcare. Vitas provides hospice and palliative care services, which are secularly growing industries. Vitas' growth is driven by an aging society that needs its services and by the need for the US government to displace expensive curative care that is inefficacious in the later years of life with compassionate palliative care. The demand for Vitas' services will occur regardless of the macroeconomic environment.

Taking Advantage of Market Dislocations: Companies with steady earnings streams can take actions that greatly benefit shareholders during times of stock price volatility through share repurchases. While most of the Fund's holdings repurchase shares, four of the holdings are buying back substantial amounts of stock. eDreams is buying stock every day near the daily limit allowed by their regulators. SECURE Waste Infrastructure Corp. bought back 19% of its stock in 2024 and is on pace to buy back another 8% in 2025. It would not surprise us if SECURE announces a substantial issuer bid soon and increases its 2025 buybacks well beyond the currently estimated 8%. Chemed and GFL are also buying back substantial amounts of stock.

While challenging economic times bring uncertainty, we are confident that the Fund's holdings can both weather the macroeconomic environment and benefit from stock price volatility. Furthermore, holding high-quality companies for the long term has proven to be a solid investment strategy. Thank you for partnering with us.

Your investment in the Ave Maria Focused Fund is appreciated.

data.title: Scheduling Linked To 10% Tariffs Causing Confusion, Angst For U.S. Importers
data.description:
Some US importers with long ocean transit times, including those who move cargo through transshipment hubs, will be on the hook for the 10% US tariffs now in place on non-Chinese goods even if those goods beat an April 5 load-by deadline to avoid the tariffs.

That’s because of a shorter-than-usual length of time set by the Trump administration encompassing the cutoff dates between when goods needed to depart their origin ports and arrive at destination ports.

And that scheduling quirk is causing consternation for importers who believed they had avoided the first salvo of tariffs by loading their cargo before the prescribed date and are now facing unexpected costs.

Don Pisano, president of American Coffee Corp., said shipments from Southeast Asia to US Gulf ports regularly take 60 days, longer than the gap between the “load-by” April 5 deadline set by the White House and the May 27 arrival date set by US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) in which the goods must be “entered for consumption.”

“It seems completely arbitrary and punitive,” Pisano told the Journal of Commerce.

Pisano pointed to another example on a different lane, a containerized shipment of coffee that left Brazil’s Port of Santos in late March on a service that went west through the Panama Canal, to Shanghai and then to the US West Coast.

“That’s not arriving until May 29, and we’ll have to pay $20,000 in tariffs because [the carrier] routed it through Shanghai,” he said.

Further, shippers whose goods were transshipped, with the final leg departing after April 5, will also be subject to the 10% US tariffs, even if the cargo shipped from origin prior to April 5. In many cases, the decision to transship is out of the control of importers.

Delay in ‘end-date’ guidance

Gabrielle Griffith, a director at BPE Global, a trade compliance consulting firm, said an end date for the in-transit exemption was not initially specified when the reciprocal tariffs were imposed April 2 through President Donald Trump’s executive order. The end-date guidance came two days later, on April 4, in a separate message in the Cargo Systems Messaging Service (CSMS), a channel CBP uses to communicate with the trade community.

Griffith said the April 4 message from CBP added the May 27 cutoff in order to, in the agency’s words, “prevent abuse of exemption,” and was reiterated by CBP on April 8.

Pisano’s contention is that most goods aiming to beat the April 5 deadline were loaded or on the water prior to either of those guidance messages being issued, as importers were racing to beat the deadline.

Griffith said the end-date provision aligns with prior CBP practices for tariff exclusions but differs from earlier executive orders issued under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEPPA) that lacked time limits.

The end date was not specified in Trump’s executive order, but CBP has some leeway to interpret the application of that order.

Transshipment confusion

The transshipment issue is potentially more vexing for importers, as CBP’s original guidance on April 4 wasn’t clear, according to a range of trade compliance experts. At issue is whether reciprocal tariffs would be exempted if goods departed their origin port before April 5, or if transshipped, departed the final leg of their ocean journey prior to April 5.

Guidance issued by CBP on May 15, which included a set of frequently asked questions, included details on how transshipment would impact tariff exemptions.

The guidance painted a scenario in which, prior to the cutoff date for the reciprocal tariff in-transit provision, US-bound cargo is loaded onto a vessel destined for a foreign port prior to shipment to the US. At that foreign port, after April 5, the cargo is transferred onto a different vessel destined for the US.

“The cargo in this scenario does not qualify for the in-transit exception for reciprocal tariffs because the US bound cargo was laden onto a vessel destined for the US after the cutoff date irrespective of when it departed from the original port of lading,” CBP wrote, responding to its own rhetorical question in the guidance.

A trade attorney who did not want to be identified told the Journal of Commerce that the May 15 guidance on transshipment is “inconsistent with previous guidance we received from CBP on the same question, so we are trying to reconcile this.”

The fact that guidance on transshipment came six weeks after the initial executive order will now impact importers for whom it wasn’t clear that such a routing wouldn’t be exempted, Pisano said.

“It took them six weeks to come up with the wrong conclusion,” he said.

data.title: CrowdStrike's Renewed Growth Opportunity Has Been Overbought
data.description:
CRWD Offers Mixed Return Prospects At Current Inflated Levels

We previously covered CrowdStrike Holdings (NASDAQ:CRWD) in March 2025, discussing why its valuations remained extremely expensive despite the recent selloff, attributed to the projected deceleration in its growth profile in the low teens against the prior guidance of over ~80%.

Despite the stable TTM Dollar-Based Retention Rates and the increased cross-selling through the expanding module adoption rates, we had warned against chasing overvalued stocks then, worsened by the growing cybersecurity competition and the higher short interest volumes.

CRWD 1Y Stock Price

Since that Hold rating, the bulls have burned the bears indeed, as CRWD also charts an eye-watering +47.8% rally compared to the wider market at +5.5%, with a similar outperformance observed in its cybersecurity peers in varying degrees.

A similar exuberance has been observed in other enterprise SaaS stocks as well, with it is apparent that demand remains robust no matter the uncertainties arising from the macroeconomic headwinds and the tariff risks, as similarly reported by numerous market players in the recent Q1'25 earning season.

The same has been reported by CRWD in the FQ1'26 earnings call, with growing subscription revenues of $1.05B (+5% QoQ/ +20.6% YoY) and Annual Recurring Revenue of $4.44B (+4.7% QoQ/ +21.6% YoY).

Given the growing top-lines, it is apparent that the cybersecurity company has been able to increasingly cross-sell to its existing consumers, as observed in the expanding module adoption rates to 48% (inline QoQ/ +4 points YoY) for six or more, 32% (inline QoQ/ +4 points YoY) for seven or more, and 22% (+1 points QoQ/ NA YoY) for eight or more modules as of the latest quarter.

The same has been observed in CRWD's gross module retention rate of 97% and the dollar-based net retention rate "in line with our expectations" in the latest quarter, compared to 97%/ 112% in FY2025 and 98%/ 119% in FY2024, respectively.

These developments underscore why Falcon Flex has worked as intended in driving higher deal value for longer periods while triggering accelerated adoption/ spend/ platform stickiness, as similarly observed in the accelerating multi-year remaining performance obligations growth to $6.8B (+4.6% QoQ/ +44.6% YoY) compared to a year ago at $4.7B (-2.1% QoQ/ +42.4% YoY):

The average Flex customer deal size is greater than $1 million in ending ARR. Two, customers commit to longer durations. The average Flex subscription length is 31 months. And three, Flex customers adopt Falcon faster. More than 75% of Flex contracts are already deployed. (Seeking Alpha)

On the other hand, despite the relatively stable subscription gross margins of 80% (inline QoQ/ -1 points YoY), it goes without saying that these have been negated by CRWD's rather aggressive go-to-market strategy, as observed in the consistently deteriorating adj operating margins to 18.2% (-2.3 points QoQ/ -6.2 YoY).

It is apparent that their "strong competitive win rates and robust pipeline for the second half of fiscal year 2026" through the Falcon Flex strategy has not been bottom-line accretive, as similarly observed in the mixed adj EPS performance of $0.73 (-29.1% QoQ/ -7.5% YoY) in FQ1'26.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

While the management has optimistically raised their FY2027 adj operating margin guidance to at least 24% and Free Cash Flow margins to over 30% (compared to the current 25.3%), we can understand why the consensus have opted to lower their forward estimates, with CRWD expected to chart a decelerating top/ bottom-line growth profile at a CAGR of +22%/ +15.8% through FY2028 (CY2027).

This is compared to the original estimates of +28.9%/ +31% (a year ago) and the 4Y historical growth profile at a CAGR of +45.8%/ +95.3%, respectively, with it naturally underscoring its impacted bottom-line growth profile, despite the management's guidance of "sequential net new ARR growth rate to be at least double over what we saw from Q1 to Q2 in the prior fiscal year" and the "margin expansion in the second half of FY '26."

CRWD Valuations

This reason is also why we believe that CRWD remains overly expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 133.18x, up drastically from the sector median of 22.90x and the August 2024 bottom of 52.69x, albeit down from the 5Y mean of 346.87x.

When comparing its FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.49x to the sector median of 1.80x and the 4Y mean of 1.51x, it is undeniable that CRWD's decelerating growth profile comes at a relatively expensive cost.

A similar conclusion may also derive after comparing to its peers, including Sentinel (S) at 0.80x and Okta (OKTA) at 1.53x, aside from Fortinet (FTNT) at 2.99x, Palo Alto (PANW) at 2.92x, and Zscaler (ZS) at 5.01x, with it underscoring why the market may have gotten overly optimistic about the cybersecurity sector's prospects as observed in the greedy stock market sentiments.

Even if we are to use another metric typically used for mature SaaS companies, namely the cash from operations of $384.1M in FQ1'26 (inline YoY) and $1.38B in FY2025 (+17.9% YoY) along with the Free Cash Flow generation of $279.4M (-13.3% YoY) and $1.07B (+15% YoY), respectively, it is apparent that CRWD remains expensive at FWD Price/ Cash Flow valuations of 82.62x.

This is compared to the sector median of 19.22x and the 5Y mean of 76.85x, with it demonstrating its unattractive investment thesis at current inflated levels, albeit somewhat aided by the increasingly rich balance sheet at a net cash position of $3.87B (+8.1% QoQ/ +30.7% YoY).

So, Is CRWD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

CRWD 5Y Stock Price

For now, CRWD has charted new heights while running away from its 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages, with it apparent that the bulls remain in the 'driver's seat.'

This reason is also why the stock is trading at a notable premium against our base-case fair value estimates of $234.50, despite the management's raised FY2026 adj EPS guidance to $3.50 at the midpoint (-10.9% YoY) and the cybersecurity peer group P/E mean of ~67x.

Based on the consensus' lowered FY2028 adj EPS estimates from $7.46 to $6.10, it is apparent that CRWD has already pulled forward nearly all of its upside potential to our base-case long-term price target of $499.80 as well.

Otherwise, a decent upside potential of +21.3% to our bull-case long-term price target of $567.90, based on the 1Y P/E mean of 93.11x, albeit with it remaining to be seen if the stock will be able to sustain its premium valuations pending the acceleration of its bottom-line growth profile ahead.

CRWD's Overbought Levels

If anything, CRWD is seemingly overbought at current levels as well, as observed in the increasingly elevated Money Flow Index and the heightened Williams %R Momentum indexes above, with it underscoring why the stock may potentially stagnate at current levels.

Otherwise, we may see the stock retrace to its prior July 2024 resistance levels of $400s ranges in the near-term, once market sentiments normalizes from current heights, with it implying a -16.1% downside from current levels.

This is worsened by CRWD's notably higher short interest volumes by +29.9% on a YoY basis, with the stock's recent gains potentially moderated by aggressive short sellers in an exuberant market environment.

As a result of the potential capital losses, we prefer to maintain our Hold rating here.

data.title: Webull: Strong Q1 Does Not Offset A 'Sell' Rating
data.description:
Two months after the SPAC of Webull Corporation (NASDAQ:BULL), the shares have consolidated, and the risk of owning this volatile stock has been reduced. Initially, the stock began trading at $16.00, and two trading days later, it reached a high of $59.21, which then rapidly declined to the point that two months later, the stock traded at $10.91, representing a -33.8% decrease from the initial trading price.

Although the company saw a notable financial improvement in Q1, I believe long-term net income trends, financial position, and valuation make me bearish on the stock of Webull, and in this analysis, I will explain my reasons to granting the stock a "sell" rating.

Business Overview

Webull is perhaps one of the closest competitors to Robinhood (HOOD). They were founded in 2016, and from my personal view, became highly popular during the pandemic, with popular financial influencers promoting them on YouTube with referral campaigns that were more appealing than the competition.

The broker offers investors an advanced trading platform with global quotes from more than 17 markets, including U.S. pre- and post-market hours. Providing trading solutions such as stocks, U.S. treasuries, ETFs, options, futures, access to the over-the-counter market, and margin trading. At the same time, they provide cash management tools for uninvested cash, recurring investing, IRAs, and money market funds.

Besides the United States, Webull has an international presence in countries such as Canada, the UK, China, Brazil, Mexico, Japan, Singapore, South Korea, and more. Yet, the vast majority of the revenues come from the United States.

Financial Trends in Q1

Over the past year, Webull experienced a notable operating leverage. In Q1, revenues grew 32% YoY to $117.4 million, while adjusted operating expenses remained fairly constant compared to a year ago, just rising 2.4%, ending at $88.7 million. And as a consequence, the adjusted operating profit expanded 22 percentage points from Q1 2024. Strong results like these are the ones that I like to see in companies. Nevertheless, the fact that adjusted expenses remained constant was mainly due to the significant decrease in marketing & branding expenses of -32.2% YoY. Also, this positive trend hasn't been the case over the past two fiscal years, and more of this will be discussed later in the analysis.

Revenue Drivers

As a predominantly commission-free broker, most of Webull's earnings are still derived from trading-related activities. For cash equity and equity options, they earn a commission from payment for order flow, where they send their orders to a market maker in exchange for a small fee. Nonetheless, for index options, they do charge $0.50 per contract, and if the number of contracts exceeds 500, they charge an oversized fee of $0.10 per contract.

In Q1 2025, Webull generated $81.7 million in trading-related revenues (72.4% of the total), with the majority coming from option revenue, followed by equity revenue, and platform and trading fees. Here, revenues grew 50% YoY, in tandem with a 45% YoY rise in customer assets.

The other revenue component is interest-related income, where Webull earns net interest income from lending customers cash, stock lending, and interest on margin financing. In Q1, this represented 33% of the business, with most of the income coming from lending clients' cash. Due to lower net interest margins resulting from an asset-sensitive balance sheet and lower interest rates, interest-related income declined by -4.3% YoY, which would have been significantly lower if customers' assets had not experienced a strong rise.

With the current landscape of higher inflation expectations from tariffs and a monetary policy where the market implies just two interest rate cuts in 2025, clients' cash as a revenue component will likely rise through the remainder of the year, as the company inboards new customers and new deposits continue rising the cash from customers.

Pipeline For Product Expansion

Webull is a broker that is experiencing strong growth. Three years ago, the company had 13.6 million registered accounts, and in Q1 2025, this number almost doubled to 24.1 million accounts. They operate in a monopolistic competition with a vast number of competitors offering similar solutions, and therefore, they do not have a strong moat compared to other market competitors. Yet, their strategy relies on entering global markets and expanding their products with solutions such as prediction markets, premium subscriptions, crypto trading, and the launch of a mobile app in Brazil, Mexico, and the Netherlands.

Risks

Although core KPIs, such as client assets, retention rates, and notional volumes, are increasing on a yearly basis, the company has experienced a slight revenue growth, and the bottom line has shown a negative trend. In 2022, the company experienced an adjusted net income gain of $91.602 million, which in 2023 declined to $35.481 million, and most recently in 2024, dropped to $9.894 million. This drop is mainly attributed to equity and option order flow rebates that passed from $278.981 million in FY 2022 to $197.070 million in FY 2024. However, as previously noted, when analyzing the Q1 results, there was a notable improvement in the bottom line compared to the same period last year, as revenues increased and expenses remained fairly constant.

From a short-term perspective, the company is doing well and growing its EPS sustainably. Yet, from a long-term perspective, considering the past three fiscal years, there is an underlying problem where the company is just able to experience a modest top-line growth, and higher expenses have caused the net income to fall substantially.

Valuation: An Overvalued Multiple

Finding a valuation method to value Webull is complex due to the following reasons.

  1. Their trailing net income of just $2.8 million, based on a market cap of more than $5 billion, will provide a multiple that is too high and unrealistic.
  2. Their shareholders' equity is negative by more than $2 billion, due to a significant amount of mezzanine equity, and therefore, multiples with book value are not possible to use.
  3. As a brokerage firm, they do not provide guidance, as most of their revenue is market-dependent.

Therefore, price-to-sales is perhaps the multiple to use in the valuation, and here, Webull has a PS ratio of 11.82x, which is higher than Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR) and The Charles Schwab (SCHW), but remains lower than Robinhood. All in all, from an absolute perspective, I believe the multiple of 11.82x price to sales is overvalued due to the low net income margin, and the modest revenue growth experienced over the past three fiscal years, despite having a strong recovery in Q1.

Takeaway: Sell

Considering all the aspects, Webull is definitely not a strong company. Their negative shareholder equity is perhaps a red flag to an investment, when it comes from the funding side, and not from an effect of aggressive repurchases, as is the case with a company such as MSCI (MSCI). At the same time, although they have robust trading tools for self-directed retail investors, the competition is high, and they do not have a strong moat. Although the last quarter was appealing and KPIs continue to experience a positive trend, combined with the valuation, this is not enough. Therefore, I will rate the shares of Webull as a "sell."

data.title: PGIM Target Date Funds Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
PGIM TARGET DATE FUNDS OVERVIEW

The PGIM Target Date Funds combine PGIM's asset management capabilities and insights into participant behavior, and Prudential's actuarial experience and leadership in pension risk transfer. Together, these provide us with a unique vantage point to assess the retirement challenge, understand its complexities, and offer a target date fund solution designed to work under the broadest set of circumstances.

The PGIM Target Date Funds are a suite of target date funds that offers:

  • A differentiated, three-stage glide path designed to solve for the right risks at the right time, by mitigating key risks participants face as they prepare for retirement.
  • A series of 12 funds managed to specific retirement dates, ranging from 2015 to 2065, as well as an Income Fund.
  • Exposure to a diversified array of asset classes, including equities, fixed income, and nontraditional asset classes like TIPS, commodities, and real estate.
  • A combination of active and passive investment management strategies designed to reduce costs while adding value in areas with greater alpha opportunities.

A DIFFERENTIATED GLIDEPATH - SOLVES FOR THE RIGHT RISKS AT THE RIGHT TIME

  1. Accumulation stage: Higher equity exposure when the greatest risk may be not taking enough risk
  2. Preservation stage: Lower equity exposure when participants may have the most to lose and don't have time to recover losses
  3. Inflation Protection stage: Focus on assets that may help preserve purchasing power for 30+ years in retirement
The Fund's allocation is ratcheted annually along the glidepath. The subadviser will review the glidepath and allocations annually, at its discretion.Note: The Fund is "ratcheted" annually to shift the Fund's allocation gradually from equity investments toward fixed income investments in accordance with the current Glidepath. Additionally, the Fund's subadviser will review the current Glidepath and the Fund's asset allocations to Underlying Funds annually to determine, in its discretion, whether the then current Glidepath allocations remain appropriate to meet the Fund's investment objective. Based on such reviews, the subadviser may, without prior notification to shareholders, make changes to the current Glidepath and/or the Fund's asset allocations as it deems appropriate to meet the Fund's investment objective in light of market and economic conditions and such other factors as it deems relevant. There is no assurance that the Fund's objective will be achieved. The Fund's allocations to the broad asset classes (equity, fixed income, and non-traditional) as set forth in the current Glidepath are not expected to vary from the Fund's allocations set forth in the strategic Glidepath by more than plus or minus 5%. The Fund's shareholder reports will set forth its actual allocations among asset classes and among Underlying Funds. The current Glidepath and strategic Glidepath are explained in more detail in the Fund's prospectus.

PGIM TARGET DATE FUNDS QUARTERLY PERFORMANCE ATTRIBUTION HIGHLIGHTS AS OF 3/31/2025

  • The first quarter was a mixed one for the capital markets, as US stocks suffered steep declines and fixed income saw modest, but positive, returns. Absolute performance was mixed for PGIM Target Date Fund investors, with the shorter-dated funds (those funds closer to their target retirement dates) delivering positive returns and the longer-dated funds (those furthest from their target retirement dates) posting modest declines. PGIM Target Date Fund performance ranged from 2.1% in the Income Fund to −0.5% in the 2070 Fund. The mix of equity exposure (domestic vs. non-US), as well as the overall allocations to stocks versus bonds, impacted absolute returns cross vintages.
  • In the first quarter U.S. stocks significantly underperformed non-U.S. stocks. U.S. large-cap stocks declined by 4.3% (S&P 500 Index) in the period, outperforming small- and mid-cap stocks (S&P MidCap 400 Index: -6.1% and Russell 2000: -9.5%). Non-U.S. stocks, however, fared better, with the developed markets returning 6.9% (MSCI EAFE Index) and emerging markets returning 2.9% (MSCI EM Index). The longer-dated funds in the series have higher equity exposure than the shorter-dated funds, and they also have a higher percentage of total equity exposure allocated to non-U.S. stocks. Therefore, while the longer-dated funds were negatively impacted by their higher exposure to large-cap U.S. stocks, their higher exposure to non-U.S. stocks was beneficial.
  • Bonds ended the quarter in positive territory, with the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index posting a 2.8% return. Inflation-protected bonds, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index, outperformed the broad U.S. bond market, with a 4.2% return. The shorter-dated funds in the series have higher overall bond exposure than the longer-dated funds, which benefited shorter-dated fund results.
  • Commodities, as measured by the Bloomberg Commodity Index, delivered a strong 8.9% return in the quarter. Real estate, as measured by the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Index, also ended the quarter in positive territory, with a 1.6% return. Commodities exposure benefited PGIM Target Date Fund results in the first quarter, as commodities outperformed both stocks and bonds.
  • The PGIM Target Date Funds had mixed results relative to their respective custom benchmarks in the quarter. While the PGIM Total Return Bond and the PGIM Quant Solutions Emerging Markets Equity funds outperformed, PGIM Jennison Small Cap, PGIM Global Real Estate and PGIM Quant 1 Solutions Commodities Funds trailed their benchmarks in the quarter.

QUARTERLY ECONOMIC OVERVIEW

The second Trump administration hit the ground running in Q1 2025, advancing aggressive policy initiatives on tariffs, immigration, executive branch reform, and cease-fires in Israel and Ukraine. Tariff policies in particular have caused significant disruption to the economic landscape. Central bank responses to tariffs are critically important; if mishandled, tariffs could lead to higher prices and dampen long-term economic growth. However, accurately gauging tariff impacts amid other economic variables is difficult, raising the risk of policy errors. The Federal Reserve (Fed) has adopted a "wait-and-see" approach, but futures markets are pricing in additional rate cuts to counteract the expected effects of tariffs. Consumer sentiment has declined, and inflation expectations have risen, reflecting the strain of the new trade policies. Although underlying economic indicators remain solid, as noted by Chair Powell, the Q1 Atlanta GDPNow forecast shows a sharp decline, signaling a potential economic slowdown. Although a recession is not yet evident in the data, the risk of a recession occurring over the next year has increased, especially if trade tensions escalate and higher tariffs are implemented.

Meanwhile, the Eurozone economy shows signs of weakness. Industrial production is struggling amid headwinds from high input costs and rising foreign competition. However, increased military spending by NATO nations could provide a stimulative economic boost, even as the European Central Bank (ECB) continues cutting rates. Japan's economy, while still growing, faces persistent inflation concerns, prompting the Bank of Japan to raise interest rates. China's economy has achieved its growth target through stimulus measures, but the challenging external economic environment poses significant risks to sustaining this momentum.

MARKET COMMENTARY

U.S. Equity

US equities posted negative performance in the first quarter of 2025, with the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) returning −4.3% on a total return basis. Despite positive returns from seven out of 11 sectors in Q1, sharp losses in Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary drove the market decline, while Energy emerged as the top performer. The Q1 market pullback has improved US equity valuations, with the forward price-to-earnings multiple on the S&P 500 easing to around 21X, down from nearly 25X at the start of the year. However, the risks of policy missteps and geopolitical shocks persist. The risk-return tradeoff between stocks and bonds is mixed, with positive equity bond correlations diminishing diversification benefits and a less favorable yield gap between bonds and stocks. For the first quarter of 2025, large caps (S&P 500: -4.3%) outperformed small caps (Russell 2000®: -9.5%) and midcaps (S&P MidCap 400−6.1%).

International Equity

International equity markets rose in Q1 of 2025, with developed and emerging markets posting respective total returns of 6.9% (MSCI EAFE) and 2.9% (MSCI EM) in US dollar terms. Developed markets saw broad-based gains, with a particularly strong quarter in Europe, where Spain, Germany, and Italy each recorded returns of more than 12%. On the emerging markets side, positive returns were driven by Poland and Brazil. Recent months have seen rotations across asset classes. US markets have lagged due to growth concerns, while international equities and emerging markets have benefited amid improved growth prospects and easing tariff fears. The dominance of growth stocks has diminished, reflecting changing market dynamics. While US equity valuations have slightly improved, international equities, particularly in emerging markets, offer more attractive valuations. If trade tensions ease and global financial conditions improve, the rotation toward these markets could gain momentum.

Non-Traditional

The US REIT is trading at an approximate 5% discount to NAV, compared to a long-term average of flat. Earnings growth is expected to accelerate in both 2025 and 2026 (roughly 6.3% and 6.7% respectively). In addition, the FFO multiple spread of the S&P 500 vs. REITs is the largest since the financial crisis. Given this attractive valuation and improving fundamentals, there may be increased private equity activity in the REIT market in 2025. Last year, the REIT market witnessed three privatizations with takeover prices represented 25−30% premium to the stocks' prior day's close, with deals across the apartments, single-family rental, and retail sectors. The Bloomberg Commodity Total Return Index increased by 8.9% in the first quarter, continuing the positive momentum from 2024. Returns in the first quarter were driven by gains in the Precious Metals (up over 18%), Softs (11.5%) and Energy (11.0%) sectors. These gains were somewhat offset by a 1.5% decline in the Grains sector.

Fixed Income

The broad US fixed income index delivered a positive return in Q1, building on the upward momentum from the end of 2024. Increased volatility across asset classes is being driven by trade policy uncertainty, fluctuating growth and inflation expectations, and central bank actions. With US debt surpassing $36 trillion, proposed tax cuts and spending plans have raised concerns about fiscal sustainability and bond market stability. Without offsetting spending cuts, rising deficits could put upward pressure on interest rates and potentially jeopardize the dollar's status as the global reserve currency. For the quarter, on a total-return basis as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, bonds returned 2.8%, lagging the 4.2% return earned by the Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index.

PERFORMANCE REVIEW: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION EFFECTS

  • The first quarter was a mixed one for the capital markets, with U.S. large cap stocks ending the quarter in negative territory and bonds in positive territory. Non-U.S. stocks (both developed and emerging markets) posted gains in the quarter. The mix of equity exposure (domestic vs. non-US), as well as the overall allocations to stocks versus bonds, impacted absolute returns from vintage to vintage.
  • U.S. large-cap stocks declined 4.3% (S&P 500 Index) in the quarter, outperforming small- and mid-cap stocks (S&P MidCap 400 Index: −6.1% and Russell 2000: -9.5%). Non-U.S. developed markets, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, returned 6.9% and emerging markets, as measured by the MSCI EM Index, returned 2.9%.
  • The longer-dated funds in the series (those furthest from their target retirement dates) have higher equity exposure than the shorterdated funds in the series, and they also have a higher percentage of total equity exposure allocated to small-cap, mid-cap and non-U.S. stocks. So, while the longer-dated funds benefited from their higher exposure to non-U.S. stocks, relative to the shorter-dated funds in the series (those closest to their target retirement dates), their higher exposure to U.S. stocks (particularly small- and mid-cap stocks) detracted.
  • The broad bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, delivered a 2.8% return. Inflation-protected bonds, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index outperformed the broad bond market, with a 4.2% first quarter return.
  • Relative to the longer-dated funds in the series, the shorter-dated funds have higher overall bond exposure, which largely benefited their performance. However, lower allocations to non-U.S. stocks, as a percentage of total equity exposure, detracted somewhat.
  • Commodities and real estate play important diversification roles in the PGIM Target Date Funds. Commodities, as measured by the Bloomberg Commodity Index, delivered a 8.9% return in the quarter. Commodities exposure benefited PGIM Target Date Fund results in the first quarter, as commodities outperformed both stocks and bonds. Real estate, as measured by the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Index, also ended the quarter in positive territory, with a 1.6% return.

PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Index Fund

  • The PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Index Fund performed in line with its benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, in the first quarter.

PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Index Fund

  • The PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Index Fund performed in line with its benchmark, the S&P MidCap 400 Index, in the first quarter.

PGIM Jennison Small-Cap Core Equity Fund

  • The PGIM Jennison Small-Cap Core Equity Fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Index, in the first quarter.
  • Security selection within the information technology and the consumer sectors detracted the most from the Fund's relative results.

International Equity

PGIM Quant Solutions International Developed Markets Index Fund

  • The PGIM Quant Solutions International Developed Markets Index Fund outperformed the MSCI EAFE Index in the first quarter. While this index fund is expected to closely track the performance of the index, due to fair market value adjustments amid volatility in the end of March, the Fund outperformed.

PGIM Quant Solutions Emerging Markets Equity Fund

  • The PGIM Quant Solutions Emerging Markets Equity Fund outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, in the first quarter.
  • From a factor perspective, all factors (value, growth, quality and top-down) contributed positively to relative performance in the quarter. From a sector perspective, a tilt toward inexpensive and high-quality stocks in the financials and materials sectors was beneficial to fund returns. From a regional perspective, investments in Saudi Arabia, Brazil and Thailand were beneficial to relative returns in the quarter.

Non-Traditional

PGIM Quant Solutions Commodity Strategies Fund

  • The PGIM Quant Solutions Commodity Strategies Fund modestly lagged its benchmark index, the Bloomberg Commodity Index, in the first quarter.
  • Weak relative results in the quarter were driven by positioning in gold and coffee.. Partially offsetting those detractors was positioning in silver. From a factor perspective, sentiment was the main detractor, with the carry factor also detracting. Risk and macro factors provided positive results.

PGIM Global Real Estate Fund

  • The PGIM Global Real Estate Fund underperformed its benchmark, the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Real Estate Index, in the first quarter.
  • Detracting from relative results was stock selection. On the whole, asset allocations decisions were modestly positive. From a regional perspective, North America delivered marginally negative relative results, where an overweight in data centers was the main detractor. Europe also detracted from relative performance in the quarter. Although stock selection in France was positive, weak security selection in Belgium and an underweight to Switzerland detracted. Finally, Asia Pacific also detracted from relative performance in the quarter, where stock selection was negative in Japan and Australia.

Fixed Income

PGIM TIPS Fund

  • The PGIM TIPS Fund modestly underperformed the Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index for the first quarter.
  • Yield curve positioning had a negligible impact on performance during the period. The duration of the Fund was tactically managed during the quarter and performance due to duration positioning also had a negligible impact on performance relative to the Index. The Fund ended the quarter with a neutral duration position versus the Index. Relative value trades detracted from performance in the quarter.

PGIM Core Conservative Bond Fund

  • The PGIM Core Conservative Bond Fund performed in line with its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the first quarter.
  • Overall sector allocation contributed to performance over the period, with an underweight to U.S. investment grade corporates as the largest contributor. Within the credit sectors, security selection in banking contributed the most to relative performance. Overall security selection detracted from relative results in the quarter, with selection in MBS detracting the most. The Fund's yield curve positioning was beneficial to the Fund's relative performance, while its duration positioning detracted.

PGIM Total Return Bond Fund

  • The PGIM Total Return Bond Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, in the fist quarter.
  • Overall security selection contributed to relative performance, with selection in U.S. high yield corporates, U.S. investment grade corporates, AAA CLOs, emerging markets high yield bonds, and non-agency MBS contributing the most. Overall sector allocation also contributed to relative results in the quarter, with an underweight to U.S. investment grade corporates and an overweight to the European sovereign credit sector providing positive contributions. I's yield curve and duration positioning contributed to performance as the yield on the intermediate and long end of the curve declined.

data.title: Adobe: An AI-Powered Software Growth Play
data.description:
Adobe Inc. (ADBE) reported better-than-expected fiscal Q2 earnings on Thursday which highlighted strong AI-powered growth in the company’s core business. Adobe benefits from growing AI product adoption of its software suite and, as a result, is seeing strong top-line expansion. Adobe raised its outlook for FY 2025 revenues amid continual AI-fueled business momentum which could push the software company's shares into a new up-leg. In my opinion, Adobe's shares are seriously undervalued with a P/E ratio of less than 20X, and I see a potentially rewarding risk profile for investors that want to take a long-term position in the software maker.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of Adobe a Strong Buy in my last work on the software marker -- "Time To Get Greedy" -- as I liked the company’s AI-focused software product suite and strong position in the creator market. Unfortunately, shares of Adobe have since dropped by ~21%. Adobe is going to continue to grow its business, especially Digital Media, and is seeing strong AI-fueled growth momentum that should get rewarded by investors sooner rather than later. With shares also being extremely attractively priced for an AI software play, I believe Adobe remains a Strong Buy.

Adobe Beat EPS And Revenue Estimates For Q2 ’25

Adobe smashed EPS and revenue expectations for the second fiscal quarter on Thursday: the software company reported adjusted earnings of $5.06 per-share which beat the average prediction by $0.09 per-share. In terms of top line, Adobe generated $5.87B in revenue, beating the consensus estimate by $70M.

Strong Top Line Momentum Continues

While companies like Nvidia (NVDA) have captured the imagination of investors in the hardware sector, there is a crop of software companies, including Adobe, that benefit from similarly strong growth momentum in the software space.

Adobe is investing heavily in AI technology in order to boost productivity and is seeing impressive top line growth. In the second fiscal quarter, Adobe generated $5.9B in revenue, showing 11% growth year-over-year. Key to Adobe's growth is Adobe Digital Media which represents 74% of the company's revenue and whose revenues increased 12% year-over-year.

Digital Media is mainly concerned with giving users tools to create, publish and monetize digital content on social media platforms. Key products in Digital Media include well-known tools such as Photoshop, Illustrator and Premiere Pro and the company has considerable customer acquisition momentum here. It is noteworthy that according to Adobe's earnings call, growth at Digital Media came especially from markets outside the U.S., such as Latin America, India and Eastern Europe.

As an AI-driven company with very strong core software products, like Photoshop, Lightroom and Adobe Assistant, Adobe was able to translate double-digit top line growth to strong gross profit growth as well. In the second fiscal quarter, Adobe generated $5.2B in gross profits, showing 11% year-over-year growth.

The software company has been able to grow its revenue and gross profits consistently over time, in part due to the tailwinds of AI. Adobe has heavily integrated artificial intelligence into its software suite and even launched entirely new AI-driven businesses such as Firefly.

Firefly is Adobe's generative AI model that offers creators, marketing professionals and other users advanced image creation tools. AI-supported tools like Firefly as well as the company's other flagship products have seen growing adoption in recent years which has led to a steady up-trend in gross profits as well as margins for Adobe.

AI Tailwinds Are Firmly Intact For Adobe's Cloud Operations

While Adobe's shares have declined more than 20% since my last coverage in September 2024, the software maker has a very strong position in the creator community... which adds to the company's moat and which should allow the company to grow its gross profit margins long term.

Companies are set to spend a record amount of money this year on generative AI technology which will ultimately benefit companies like Adobe that are at the forefront of integrating state-of-the-art AI into their product offerings.

According to Gartner, generative AI spending is set to soar 76.4% year-over-year in FY 2025, driven chiefly by the Services, Devices and Software categories. With total spending estimated to reach as high as $643.9B, Adobe is going to remain a highly relevant AI software play going forward.

Guidance For FY 2025

Adobe guided for $23.5-23.6B in revenue for the current fiscal year which implies a year-over-year growth rate of 9%. The software maker also raised its revenue target for FY 2025, by $125M at the mid-point, mainly because of strong customer acquisition momentum that the company experiences as a result of its success with AI tools.

Adobe’s Valuation

I guess what appeals to me more than anything with regard to Adobe is that the AI software maker’s shares are very sensibly valued. Shares of Adobe are priced at a very moderate forward price-to-earnings ratio of 18.1X which I believe undervalues the company's earnings potential in the red-hot AI software market.

Compared to other AI-focused software plays, such as Salesforce (CRM), Oracle (ORCL) or Microsoft (MSFT), Adobe has actually a very sensible valuation: the average AI/software company here is trading for a P/E ratio of 23.8X. This means that currently Adobe is trading at a 23% discount to its 3-year average price-to-earnings ratio as well as a 24% discount to the industry group average P/E.

Since Adobe is expected to have stronger-than-average earnings growth prospects -- Adobe's 15.2% projected EPS growth rate compares to an industry average rate of 14.3% -- I don't see any reason for this relative undervaluation. In my opinion, Adobe could revalue to a forward (FY 2026) earnings multiplier of ~24X which implies 33% upside revaluation potential and a fair value of around $550 per-share.

Risks With Adobe

Through the leveraging of AI capabilities, small start-ups can now compete effectively against established software companies like Adobe that have large subscription bases and big moats. Therefore, AI could either help Adobe accelerate its business or suffer growing competition as a result. What would change my mind about Adobe is if the software maker were to see slowing growth in its AI-centered products or saw its gross margin trend reverse.

Final Thoughts

Adobe is a very reasonably priced software play with considerable AI exposure in its core business. The company generated double-digit top-line second fiscal quarter. AI-powered growth is at the very heart of Adobe’s monetization strategy, which should lead to healthy expansion in margins as well as continual strength in customer acquisition.

In my opinion, Adobe’s 18.1X P/E ratio is not fully representative of the underlying strength of the platform’s software business or its profitability. Since AI spending trends indicate that more companies (and consumers) are set to use more AI products in the future, the outlook for Adobe is very bright as well.

data.title: Hercules Capital: Growing Troubles Within Portfolio Warrants Caution
data.description:
Overview

Business Development Companies continue to be my favorite place to get a mixture of capital appreciation and high income. However, the current macroeconomic environment has reduced the appeal of the sector. Many BDCs have started to experience a drop in portfolio quality and lower earnings as a result of the higher interest rate environment. I recently published an article titled 'Warning To All BDC Investors', which I would recommend you go back to read for a more specific breakdown of my concerns. I was lucky enough to initiate a position in Hercules Capital (NYSE:HTGC) around the $12 per share mark, and recently sold out of that position at around $19 per share.

With enough of a decline, HTGC is one of the high-quality BDCs that I would initiate a position in again if valuation was attractive. On a YTD basis, HTGC's price is still down nearly 10%, but I have concerns that the price can continue its slide towards $16 if interest rates aren't cut. The price still remains above its average price to NAV level, indicating that HTGC is still overvalued. However, it's hard to come across a high-quality BDC at a discount opportunity, so there's also a chance that we've already seen the worst and conditions improve from here. Non-accruals have remained basically flat, with only 1 new addition.

If internal performance does improve, there's certainly a chance that we could see a turnaround. Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that HTGC's price has declined by about 8.2%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return lands at a positive 1.2%. The main appeal of holding a position in HTGC is the generous starting dividend yield of about 10.5%. This double-digit yield makes it an attractive option for investors who have an objective of creating a sizable stream of dividend income. So let's start by taking a look at the current risk profile for HTGC.

Risk Profile

One of the biggest sources of risk that I am cautious about is the growing demand for private credit investments, such as BDCs. Demand is growing from both institutional and retail investors, which may seem like a great thing for the long-term growth of the sector. However, I worry that the demand outpaces the amount of high-quality deals that are available in the market. Sourcing highly profitable and sustainable deals in this higher interest rate environment is challenging enough.

This increased demand means there is increased competition for fund managers. I interpret this increased competition as many different hands reaching into the same pot of higher-quality deals. Ideally, BDCs want to continue growing their base assets, NAV per share, and net investment income year over. I fear that some BDCs may start to loosen their underwriting criteria just to secure some new growth opportunities, which can lead to higher non-accruals in the long term if interest rates remain elevated.

I tend to monitor the changes in non-accrual rates every quarter for all of the BDCs in my portfolio. I believe that non-accruals provide us with an insight into management's underwriting ability, and the portfolio's interest rate sensitivity, and help us get an idea of how management addresses its issues. Non-accruals represent the rate of borrowers who are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. At the time of my last coverage, non-accruals amounted to 1.7% of cost and 0.5% of fair portfolio value.

The latest earnings call revealed that non-accruals have remained mostly flat, with non-accruals of 1.8% of cost and 0.5% of fair portfolio value. This can be attributed to one additional company being added to non-accrual status. Management stated that they feel confident in the resilience of their portfolio for the remainder of the year. However, I wanted to highlight one interesting point during that earnings call. Management states that many companies are freezing decision-making as they await better certainty about the outlook of the macroeconomic environment. I believe that this may be the source of risk that can drive non-accruals higher at some point.

What we've noticed, particularly over the last 30 to 60 days, is that companies are sort of freezing decision-making given the uncertainty. So decision-making with respect to potential growth investments, decision-making with respect to exploring strategic options, decision-making with respect to capital raise, those discussions, those decisions have slowed down. And I think a lot of companies are just looking for clarity and looking for certainty from a policy perspective. We think that will change hopefully relatively quickly, but that's something that we are watching pretty closely. - Scott Bluestein, Chief Executive Officer

While non-accruals don't paint the entire picture, I wanted to provide some points of reference. Therefore, I listed some of the non-accrual rates of peer BDCs below with their respective total return over the last twelve months. The rest of the BDC sector has seen a slight uptick in non-accruals, which is just the nature of having a portfolio of debt investments that are structured on a floating rate basis.

  • Main Street Capital (MAIN): 1.7% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • PennantPark Investment (PNNT): 1.5% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX): 1.2% non-accrual rate at fair value.

We can see that HTGC has underperformed its peers over the last twelve months, indicating that there could be some other flaw related to its portfolio strategy. I believe it is related to the growing amount of PIK interest income, which I will circle back to later.

Portfolio Strategy

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, HTGC has total investments at the fair value of $3.9M that are allocated across a diverse range of industries. Looking at the pie chart breakdown below, you may strain your eyes trying to figure out what color matches what percentage. I saved you the effort and provided a breakdown of the top five industries below.

  • Software: 32.2%
  • Drug Discovery & Development: 28.5%
  • Healthcare Services, Other: 15.9%
  • Consumer & Business Products: 9.6%
  • Electronics & Computer Hardware: 4.3%

What I gathered from the breakdown is that a significant portion of HTGC's assets are concentrated in the top three sectors, which make up 76.6% of assets when combined. The software and healthcare sectors tend to be more volatile and sensitive to interest rate changes. Since HTGC is one of the few BDCs that include venture debt as part of its portfolio, the risk in these sectors is elevated. Unfortunately, the higher interest rate environment has slowed down the appeal of venture lending due to the elevated interest expenses.

98% of HTGC's portfolio is structured on floating rate debt investments, which allowed for a greater amount of interest income to be collected at one point. While this may have been beneficial in prior years, the burden of the higher cost of debt is beginning to weigh down on borrowers. For reference, the federal funds rate still remains near its decade highs, despite a few rate cuts happening during Q4 of 2024. The Fed has decided to leave rates unchanged during their latest meeting, and I anticipate that interest rates will remain elevated for the remainder of 2025. The full impact of President Trump's tariffs is still to be determined, and the Fed will likely continue to monitor inflation data over the next few months.

On a more positive note, 90.9% of the portfolio is structured on first-lien senior secured loans. First-lien senior secured debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure, which means this debt has the highest priority for repayment. This reduces the chance that HTGC loses all invested capital in a bad transaction. In a scenario where a borrower defaults on their loan and has to liquidate assets, HTGC's loans will be entitled to repayment before other forms of debt.

Lastly, HTGC's consistent performance and track record of success have caused the price to trade at one of the highest premiums to NAV valuations in recent years. As we can see below, the price currently trades at a premium to NAV of 1.74x, indicating that entry here may not be ideal. Wall Street analysts have an average price target of $20.11 per share, indicating a potential upside of nearly 11%. However, I believe the price may retract back down to the $17 range before making its way back up, due to some underlying concerns.

Q1 Earnings — Worrisome PIK Interest Income

HTGC reported its Q1 earnings at the beginning of May and the results were a bit mixed in my opinion. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.45 per share, which was a 10% reduction from the prior year's Q1 earnings. Pre-tax net investment income landed at $77.4M, which was also a slight 2% decrease from the prior year's amount of $79.1M. Total net operating expenses did slightly decrease by 1% to $42M, from the prior year's total net operating expenses of $42.3M.

However, the thing that really stood out to me was the massive 36% increase in PIK (payment-in-kind) interest income. PIK interest income increased to $13.4M, up from the prior year's total of $9.8M. This is an issue for me because PIK interest income can be defined as interest that is accrued but not actually paid in cash from a borrower. This means that borrowers are having a more difficult time paying their interest in cash. Therefore, HTGC is allowing payments to be deferred and adding the interest to the principal of their loans instead. HTGC is legally allowed to categorize this as interest income, even though no actual cash was received.

Granted, HTGC may be excused by many optimistic investors, since their whole investment approach exposes the BDC to many cash-constrained businesses in the early startup stage. However, I tend to shy away from increased risk profiles like this because I believe this issue can build in the background and surprise investors when conditions become much worse. If PIK's interest income is increasing at this rate, it could mean that the distribution will eventually be in jeopardy of a cut. Imagine a scenario where interest rate cuts do not happen for another twelve months; how will HTGC be able to offset this deterioration if there are a limited number of deals in the market?

Despite HTGC's net assets slightly increasing above $2B, the NAV per share continues to decline quarter over quarter. A declining NAV is one of the clearest indications that a problem may be arising.

  • Q1 2024 NAV: $11.63 per share
  • Q2 2024 NAV: $11.43 per share
  • Q3 2024 NAV: $11.43 per share
  • Q4 2024 NAV: $11.66 per share
  • Q1 2025 NAV: $11.55 per share

As we can see below, the net interest margin percentage continues to shrink as a result of the deterioration. Profitability is being threatened by a higher cost of funding and a lower yield collected from its portfolio. While the margins still remain healthy, I just thought it was noteworthy to mention the trend of its margin.

On a more positive note, HTGC was able to originate total gross debt and equity commitments of over $1B and gross fundings of approximately $539M during the quarter. This was the second-highest gross funding that HTGC was able to achieve in its entire history. Even after sales and repayments, the portfolio saw a net growth of about $270M during the quarter. Despite the troubling increase in PIK interest income, I do like the fact that management seems to be very active in trying to resolve weak areas.

The focus of our origination efforts in Q1 and quarter-to-date Q2 was on maintaining a disciplined approach to capital deployment with an emphasis on diversification and avoidance of certain sectors that we believe will be more challenged in the current operating environment. Our Q1 originations activity placed a slightly greater emphasis on technology companies versus life sciences companies. - Scott Bluestein, Chief Executive Officer

Dividend Support

According to the most recently declared quarterly base distribution of $0.40 per share, the current dividend yield sits at around 8.8%. Management also declared a supplemental distribution of $0.07 per share over the last quarter, bringing the total payout to $0.47 per share. As previously mentioned, net investment income for the last quarter amounted to $0.45 per share. So while this does still fully support the base distribution, I wouldn't expect a significant number of supplementals to be paid over the next twelve months.

HTGC has built a strong reputation around the consistency of its dividends. Looking at the visual below, we can see how the payouts have remained fairly stable and reliable, despite shifts in the overall macro environment. Therefore, I anticipate that management will do their best to keep the distribution the same and avoid any cuts. As it currently stands, net investment income levels support the base distribution with a coverage rate of 112.5%. Since HTGC recently allocated a large amount of capital towards new investment activity, I anticipate this coverage margin to improve once these new investments start to materialize. Therefore, I am not really concerned about a dividend cut at this moment.

Even if there aren't any formal base dividend raises or supplementals in the future, this consistency must be admired. That's because long-term investors can essentially create their own dividend growth by reinvesting a portion of the income received from HTGC.

It's worth mentioning that the distributions received from HTGC are made up of net investment income. Therefore, all distributions received will be classified as ordinary dividend income. Based on the amount of capital invested, specific tax bracket, or desired income level, a position in HTGC may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset any potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on HTGC at this moment due to the premium valuation and some underlying concerns about the portfolio quality. Although non-accruals remained relatively unchanged, PIK interest income has increased. This could be an indication that underlying borrowers are having a hard time making the cash interest payments needed. If interest rates remain elevated for longer than anticipated, this could cause the quality of the portfolio to worsen. However, management allocated a large amount of capital towards new investments, which could materialize into stronger earnings throughout the remainder of the year. The distribution is still supported by earnings, alleviating concerns about a cut.

data.title: Shurgard Self Storage: Attractive Growth Profile, But Too Expensive
data.description:
Introduction

Shurgard Self Storage (OTCPK:SSSAF) is a Belgium based REIT focusing on self-storage facilities across Europe. When the company initially completed its IPO, I decided against participating, as I just didn’t see Europe following the ‘American style’ of using self-storage services. But it appeared to have been a case of ‘build, and you will be able to find customers’ as Shurgard (and other self storage REITs in Europe) have done quite well. As the stock has come off its 52-week high, I wanted to have another look at Shurgard to see if it now meets my investment criteria.

Shurgard Self Storage has its primary listing in Belgium, where it is trading on Euronext Brussels with SHUR as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume is almost 42,000 shares. The current market capitalization is approximately 3.5B EUR, as the REIT has approximately 98.5M shares outstanding. I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article.

The REIT continues to grow

Shurgard does not provide detailed quarterly financial statements, but its Q1 trading update clearly shows the direction the REIT is heading in, while the full-year guidance remains very strong as well.

During the first quarter of the year, the revenue increased by approximately 19.5% and thanks to additional efficiency and economies of scale, this allowed the NOI to increase by 20.4% to 64.6% as the NOI margin increased from 57.5% to 57.9%. The EPRA earnings, which are comparable to the FFO result in North America, came in at 35.7M EUR or 36 cents per share. That’s an increase of just 3.2% compared to the same quarter a year before, but that is related to the increased debt level and interest expenses as the REIT remains committed to its substantial investment program.

As the table above shows, the REIT has almost four dozen expansion projects in the pipeline for a total investment of 515M EUR. Of that amount, about a third will be spent this year. The addition of 212,000 square meters will add about 13% to the net rentable square meters that the REIT currently has available.

The outlook for 2025 remains stable: the REIT expects an 11% NOI increase (this appears low given the sharp increase in the first quarter, but the REIT’s H2 2024 was much stronger than the first half after completing an acquisition) and a 0.5% improvement of the underlying EBITDA margin. The net interest expenses are expected at approximately 50M EUR.

This means we can now use this guidance in combination with the 2024 results to derive the anticipated earnings. We know the 2024 NOI was approximately 268M EUR, and an 11% increase results in an anticipated NOI of 298M EUR.

Last year, the G&A expenses were 28M EUR, and after deducting about 35M EUR in G&A and the 50M EUR in anticipated interest expenses, the pre-tax income should come in at around 213M EUR. Applying the average tax rate of 18.5% would result in an attributable FFO of 174M EUR and divided over the 98.5M shares outstanding, this implies EPRA earnings of 1.77 EUR per share. That’s in line with the consensus estimates.

The earnings should continue to increase at a mid to high single digit percentage, and I expect the REIT to reach EPRA earnings of close to 2 EUR per share in 2027.

Is this how Public Storage wants to increase its European footprint?

The REIT has a relatively small free float of just 28%, and two shareholders own almost 70% of the capital. As you can see below, the New York State Common Retirement Fund owns just over a third of the stock, while Public Storage (PSA) owns almost 35% of Shurgard’s share.

Shurgard went public in 2018 when it was a private company mainly held by the New York retirement fund (with a stake of 50.9%) and Public Storage which owned 48.9%.

The question now is what the main shareholders would prefer to do. At the current share price of approximately 36 EUR, Shurgard is trading at close to 20 times its FFO which is in line with its peers (Public Storage is trading at a forward FFO multiple for 2026 of 17 while smaller peers with a market cap of respectively $10B and $4B (the latter is more in line with Shurgard’s market capitalization) CubeSmart (CUBE) and National Storage Affiliates (NSA) are trading at forward multiples of 16 and 14 respectively). Applying a multiple of 15 times earnings to Shurgard would result in a target price of just 28 EUR by the end of 2026.

It could make sense for Public Storage to take Shurgard private again, perhaps together with the New York State retirement fund, but at the current valuation, the stock may be a bit in limbo. Shurgard is perhaps a bit too expensive to try to acquire it (as a 20-25% premium would push the multiples to 23–24 times next year’s FFO result) but it’s also not expensive enough to consider selling given its growth profile and the fact that PSA is trading at similar (and even slightly higher) multiples. Public Storage may continue to use Shurgard as its platform to build out a European subsidiary, but I doubt the US-based REIT has any plans to take Shurgard private again.

Investment thesis

While I underestimated the demand for self-storage units in Europe, I’m still not planning to go long anytime soon. At the current share price of just over 36 EUR per share, the self-storage REIT is trading at about 20 times next year’s earnings and even if it reaches the 2 EUR per share in FFO result by the end of 2027, the stock still wouldn’t be cheap at 18 times the FFO result.

As such, I remain on the sidelines and am a ‘hold’ on Shurgard Self Storage.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TEAF: Divesting Private Investments As Fund Transforms
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Tortoise Sustainable and Social Impact Term Fund (NYSE:TEAF) is a closed-end fund that holds a unique portfolio split between private and public investments, primarily in the energy space. However, after a strategic review of the fund due to a lack of performance over the years and activist pressure, they decided to divest the private holdings.

Since announcing that shift in March of this year, they've made significant progress on reducing the private investment sleeve. That said, the fund's performance since announcing the transition has been pressured. This was due to an overall volatile April and a weaker general environment for energy investments.

Since our last update, the fund's total returns have been slightly positive. The update came only a week or so before the announcement of the results of their strategic review.

TEAF Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.99
  • Discount/Premium: -12.51%
  • Distribution Yield: 9.25%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.98%
  • Leverage: 14.88%
  • Managed Assets: $211.655 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation date around March 27, 2031)

TEAF was launched with the goal of "attractive total return potential with emphasis on current income and uncorrelated assets." Additionally, "access to differentiated direct investments in essential assets" and "investments in intangible, long-lived assets and services." TEAF is also targeting a "positive social and economic impact."

The fund is leveraged, and that increases the potential for higher returns but also for amplifying downside moves. Overall, the fund is rather mildly leveraged relative to most CEFs. Still, it also adds further expenses through the borrowing costs. That pushes the fund's total expense ratio to 2.75%.

Private Divestment As Fund Repositions

They announced their "next steps" after the strategic review of TEAF on March 17, 2025. The announcement was that they'd be looking to divest most of their private investments as they felt that was in the best interest of shareholders.

As part of this review, TEAF engaged Lucid Capital Markets to facilitate the sale of its private credit, social infrastructure, assets. The fund is currently under contract to divest these holdings, as it was determined that divestment is in the best interest of shareholders. As of March 17, 2025, these private credit assets comprised 29% of TEAF’s portfolio. The sale of the private credit assets is expected to close on or about March 20th generating proceeds of $55.9 million.

In addition, TEAF engaged Marathon Capital to facilitate the sale of its fixed renewable assets. The fund is currently under exclusivity for the sale of the majority of the private renewables portfolio, with the transaction expected to close in Q2 2025. The proceeds from the sales will be reinvested in TEAF’s listed energy infrastructure holdings. The sustainable infrastructure holdings will remain unchanged.

Private investments can be quite mixed; they can provide a significant boost in overall results in an ideal world, but just like the public world, not everything is a hit. Further, the process brings with the the "true value" of the underlying portfolio. As private level 3 securities, the valuations of these investments are generally a best estimate based on various valuation techniques.

At the end of the day, though, they are really only worth what someone is willing to pay for them—and that price tag isn't figured under a negotiation that is settled between the parties.

Since the announcement of the divestment of their private holdings, mostly linked to renewable energy or credit investments, TEAF has actually performed a bit better than the traditional peers on a total NAV return basis. That includes the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) and Tortoise Essential Energy Fund (TPZ), a newly formed ETF with the merger of its previous CEF sister funds. TEAF had also outperformed its remaining CEF sister fund, Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corp (TYG).

However, on the other side of this, it significantly underperformed its renewable-focused peer ETFs, iShares Global Clean Energy ETF (ICLN) and Invesco Solar ETF (TAN). So it appears that they were divesting their mostly renewable-focused private investments at a time that looks like it was advantageous to be allocated to those types of holdings.

To be fair, they still hold some of their private investments, as they account for 17% of the fund's portfolio as of May 31, 2025. These are some of the largest renewable investments they had through the Renewable Holdco positions.

Further, we had an incredibly volatile April 2025 that would have mired the performance of TEAF and its peers through this period as well. That can make it a bit more cloudy to come up with a solid conclusion on whether they were getting the valuations they had assigned to their private positions or not. In the next report, due in a couple of months, we might be able to get a better idea of how accurate the valuations might have been.

TEAF has also seen its discount narrow since our last update, and it has narrowed quite materially from the deep-discounted levels we saw in 2023/2024.

Ultimately, only time will tell if this was the correct decision to go back to a more traditional public infrastructure-invested portfolio. Albeit, one that is still focused on "sustainable" infrastructure that can make it a little bit more unique relative to its sister funds and peers.

Impact On Monthly Distribution

Even after the fund is repositioning itself through a transformation, the fund has continued to pay the same monthly distribution. At the $0.09 per month, that works out to a distribution rate of 9.25%—and thanks to a significant discount to NAV, the fund's NAV rate comes to 8.09%.

In terms of distribution coverage, we may actually see net investment income decline. This would be due to the divestment of its private credit portfolio, which would have generated a significant portion of the total investment income.

However, the fund still always required meaningful capital gains to support the payout as well. NII for fiscal 2024 came to only 37%, and including the MLP return of capital distributions, distributable cash flow coverage came to 47%.

In this case, from the top ten, we see that now Energy Transfer LP (ET) has moved from a 2.7% allocation to 9.1%. MPLX LP (MPLX) also saw a significant increase from 2.7% to 4.9%. ET pays a distribution rate of 7.18%, and MPLX pays a distribution rate on its units that currently works out to 7.41%.

With this transition, it would appear that the managers are going to be trading in interest payments for distributions characterized as return of capital. That could result in the NII coverage decreasing, but DCF coverage not seeing as significant an impact. Therefore, the overall coverage of the distribution in terms of what could be considered relatively stable recurring cash flow versus the need for capital appreciation may not see a massive shift—that's despite the overall transformation of the portfolio.

Conclusion

TEAF has made the decision to exit much of its private portfolio after its strategic review came to a conclusion. The manager believes this is in the best interest of the shareholders over the long term as the fund becomes more focused on public securities in the infrastructure space. Of course, only time will tell if this is ultimately the right decision that will lead to long-term success. This also means that TEAF isn't so unique anymore, though they still hold what would be considered a rather sizeable tilt toward sustainable infrastructure through primarily its Renewable Holdco investments.

For now, TEAF remains an interesting fund in terms of what is still a rather sizable discount and attractive distribution yield. Saba Capital still owns a sizeable stake in the fund at around 11.33%. They actually even bumped up their allocation during the broader April market swoon.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
